what_it_does: This file registers a flow row named registry_generation that wraps an external rg10_run.run_batch pipeline. It declares metadata in a FLOW dict and provides a run() function that adjusts sys.path and delegates to run_batch with a time-budget parameter, returning its result.
observations: The module is dominated by a long docstring framing the file as a proven instance of the GC2 grounded-chain pattern.
A top-level FLOW dict holds id, label, description, params, and a proposes_only flag.
The run function mutates sys.path to include /home/tim/company/build-prep/cognition-self-improvement before importing rg10_run.
run is a thin wrapper: it converts time_budget_s to int and returns run_batch(int(time_budget_s)).
The flow claims to be resume-safe and proposal-only, with downstream operator approval.
standouts: Dense manifest-style docstring with GC1/GC2 law language and a no-fiction pledge.
Hardcoded absolute filesystem path inside run().
proposes_only flag explicitly separating proposal generation from approval.
Dynamic import of the actual implementation inside the run function.
conventions: Follows the flow-row convention of exposing a FLOW metadata dict and a run() entry point.
Breaks portability by hardcoding a user-specific absolute path.
Breaks explicit dependency conventions by doing a runtime sys.path insert and import.
concerns: Hardcoded /home/tim/... path makes the file environment-specific and non-portable.
sys.path.insert inside run has side effects and may shadow other modules.
No input validation or error handling for time_budget_s or run_batch failures.
The real registry-generation logic is outside this file, so this file is not self-contained.
notes: A self-describing flow adapter that exposes an external registry-filling pipeline as a single callable, bounded, proposal-only step.
questions: What contract does rg10_run.run_batch expect and what does its returned dict contain?
How is resume-safe state actually persisted in .build/rg10/?
What are the closed vocabularies, deterministic refcheck floor, and accuracy jury in concrete terms?
How does the downstream operator approve gate consume the artifacts produced here?
Is the /home/tim path stable across deployment environments?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to provide a single easy call for a grounded, bounded, proposal-only registry chain. Actual content is a metadata declaration and a thin wrapper that delegates to an external script, which matches as a flow registration but leaves all chain logic elsewhere.
apparent_themes: Grounded generation with explicit no-fiction constraints
Delegation to an external proven pipeline
intention_signals: It is trying to package a complex, trustworthy registry-filling pipeline as a simple one-call flow while making its limits and operator controls explicit.
novelty: The file presents itself as a registered flow row using GC1/GC2 pattern language and a proposes_only flag, with a strong no-invention contract.
contribution: A standardized flow entry point and metadata record that exposes rg10_run.run_batch under a named, bounded, proposal-only contract, making the external pipeline discoverable and callable as a flow row.
summary_for_embedding: A registered flow row that wraps the rg10_run.run_batch registry-filling pipeline, exposing a bounded, resume-safe, proposal-only grounded mockup-to-dossier chain via a FLOW metadata dict and a run(time_budget_s) function.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a single easy call for the grounded registry generation chain while keeping approval downstream and preventing ungrounded invention.

symbols.does: Invokes the rg10_run.run_batch pipeline with the given time budget.
symbols.description: run(time_budget_s: int=420) -> dict mutates sys.path to include the build-prep cognition-self-improvement directory, dynamically imports rg10_run, and returns the result of run_batch(int(time_budget_s)); it is the executable entry point declared by the FLOW row.
